name: The order of a G-symplectic method
subsection_title: The order of a G-symplectic method
statement_text:
The following method has order 4 and stage order 2:

proof_text:
Proof. Write ξ1 , ξ2 as the representations of y1 , y2 and η1 , η2 to represent
the stages. The stages have to be found recursively and only the converged
values are given in Table 534(I), which shows the sequence of quantities
occurring in the calculation. The values given for ξi are identical to those
for Eξi , i = 1, 2, verifying that the order is 4. Furthermore ηi (t) = E (ci ) (t),
i = 1, 2, for r(t) ≤ 2, showing stage order 2.
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
The theorem presents a general linear method defined by a partitioned matrix $\begin{bmatrix} A & U \\ B & V \end{bmatrix}$, where $A$, $U$, $B$, $V$ are coefficient matrices. The context discusses $G$-symplectic methods and their order analysis. A $G$-symplectic method preserves symplectic structure when applied to Hamiltonian problems, with a specific matrix $G$. The starting procedure involves a Runge--Kutta method (502b). Method (525d) is an example of a $G$-symplectic method.

equations.content: A Runge-Kutta method used to motivate the construction of the new method, referenced in the starting procedure
An example of a G-symplectic method mentioned as the second example in the subsection

preamble: The theorem presents a general linear method defined by a partitioned matrix [A U; B V], where A, U, B, V are coefficient matrices. The context discusses G-symplectic methods and their order analysis, with references to specific methods like (525d) and starting procedures involving Runge-Kutta methods.
